section.title: Laborers in the Vineyard
section.sectionRationale: The parable of the vineyard workers illustrates the reversal Jesus just spoke, the last will be first, exposing the grumbling heart that resents grace. Then, a third time, Jesus foretells His death, corrects a grasp for status with the definition of greatness as service, and heals two blind men who see and follow.
section.thesis: God's kingdom runs on His sovereign generosity, not on human merit or seniority, so the grasping heart must be undone; greatness is service, and the Son of Man Himself comes not to be served but to give His life as a ransom for many.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the parable of the workers and the generous landowner; the third passion prediction; the request for thrones answered by the call to servanthood and the ransom saying; and the healing of two blind men outside Jericho.

verses.text: “For the kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out early in the morning to hire workers for his vineyard.
He agreed to pay them a denarius for the day and sent them into his vineyard.
About the third hour he went out and saw others standing in the marketplace doing nothing.
‘You also go into my vineyard,’ he said, ‘and I will pay you whatever is right.’
So they went. He went out again about the sixth hour and the ninth hour and did the same thing.
About the eleventh hour he went out and found still others standing around. ‘Why have you been standing here all day long doing nothing?’ he asked.
‘Because no one has hired us,’ they answered. So he told them, ‘You also go into my vineyard.’
When evening came, the owner of the vineyard said to his foreman, ‘Call the workers and pay them their wages, starting with the last ones hired and moving on to the first.’
The workers who were hired about the eleventh hour came and each received a denarius.
So when the original workers came, they assumed they would receive more. But each of them also received a denarius.
On receiving their pay, they began to grumble against the landowner.
‘These men who were hired last worked only one hour,’ they said, ‘and you have made them equal to us who have borne the burden and the scorching heat of the day.’
But he answered one of them, ‘Friend, I am not being unfair to you. Did you not agree with me on one denarius?
Take your pay and go. I want to give this last man the same as I gave you.
Do I not have the right to do as I please with what is mine? Or are you envious because I am generous?’
So the last will be first, and the first will be last.”

units.label: The passion foretold a third time

verses.text: As Jesus was going up to Jerusalem, He took the twelve disciples aside and said,
“Look, we are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered over to the chief priests and scribes. They will condemn Him to death
and will deliver Him over to the Gentiles to be mocked and flogged and crucified. And on the third day He will be raised to life.”

units.label: Greatness is service
units.note: the Son of Man came to give His life

verses.text: Then the mother of Zebedee’s sons came to Jesus with her sons and knelt down to make a request of Him.
“What do you want?” He inquired. She answered, “Declare that in Your kingdom one of these two sons of mine may sit at Your right hand, and the other at Your left.”
“You do not know what you are asking,” Jesus replied. “Can you drink the cup I am going to drink?” “We can,” the brothers answered.
“You will indeed drink My cup,” Jesus said. “But to sit at My right or left is not Mine to grant. These seats belong to those for whom My Father has prepared them.”
When the ten heard about this, they were indignant with the two brothers.
But Jesus called them aside and said, “You know that the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their superiors exercise authority over them.
It shall not be this way among you. Instead, whoever wants to become great among you must be your {{diakonos|servant}},
and whoever wants to be first among you must be your slave,
just as the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give His life as a {{lytron|ransom}} for many.”
As they were leaving Jericho, a large crowd followed Him.
And there were two blind men sitting beside the road. When they heard that Jesus was passing by, they cried out, “Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!”
The crowd admonished them to be silent, but they cried out all the louder, “Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!”
Jesus stopped and called them. “What do you want Me to do for you?” He asked.
“Lord,” they answered, “let our eyes be opened.”
Moved with {{splanchnizomai|compassion}}, Jesus touched their eyes, and at once they received their sight and followed Him.

diakonos.gloss: A servant, one who waits on others, the root of the word deacon. Jesus overturns worldly greatness: in His kingdom the great one is the servant, and the first is the slave, on the pattern of the serving Son of Man.

lytron.gloss: A ransom, the price paid to release a captive or slave. The Son of Man gives His life as a ransom in the place of many, the heart of the gospel: His death purchases the freedom of those held by sin and death.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion from the depths. It is Jesus' gut-level mercy for the two blind men whom the crowd would silence, the same compassion that runs through His whole ministry.

basic.context: Jesus tells of a landowner who hires workers through the day and pays them all the same wage, from the first at dawn to the last at the eleventh hour. The all-day workers grumble; the owner answers that he has been just to them and is free to be generous. Jesus foretells His death a third time, in fuller detail. When James and John's mother asks for the top thrones, Jesus redefines greatness as service and reveals He came to give His life as a ransom. Then He heals two blind beggars who call Him Son of David.
basic.hermeneutics: The parable illustrates the saying that brackets it, the last will be first. It is not about labor economics but about grace: God gives the kingdom freely, and the merit-minded resent it. Notice the contrast: right after Jesus foretells His self-giving death, the disciples grasp for status, and He corrects them with His own example, the ransom.
basic.originalLanguages: Greatness in the kingdom belongs to the diakonos, the servant. The Son of Man gives His life as a lytron, a ransom, in the place of many. And He heals the blind men because He is moved with splanchnizomai, compassion from the depths.
basic.typology: The vineyard is a familiar image of God's people. The cup Jesus will drink is the cup of God's judgment the prophets spoke of. The ransom fulfills the servant of Isaiah 53 who pours out His life for many.
basic.christ: Jesus is the generous landowner's Lord, the Son of Man who will be mocked, flogged, and crucified, and the Servant who gives His life as a ransom for many. He is the Son of David whose compassion opens blind eyes and draws the healed to follow Him.
basic.god: God is free and generous, giving grace as He pleases and refusing to let human bookkeeping bind Him. He is just to all and lavish beyond desert, and He prepares places in His kingdom according to His own will.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 foretells the servant who pours out His life for many. Isaiah 5 pictures God's vineyard. Philippians 2 shows Christ taking the form of a servant. Mark 10 parallels the ransom saying and the blind man of Jericho.
basic.oneStory: As Jesus climbs toward Jerusalem, He unmasks the grasping heart that resents grace and craves status, and He sets against it His own path: the Son of Man who serves and gives His life as a ransom for many. The kingdom comes by His self-giving, not our merit, and the last are made first. The blind who cry for mercy see and follow, a picture of all who receive the ransom by faith.

context.literary: The parable is bracketed by the reversal saying of 19:30 and 20:16, the last will be first, and it answers Peter's earlier what will there be for us. It is followed by the third and fullest passion prediction, then by the disciples' status-seeking, which Jesus meets with the ransom saying, the theological summit of the section. The healing of the blind men, who see and follow, closes the journey narrative before the entry into Jerusalem.
context.historical: A denarius was a day's wage, enough to feed a family for a day; day laborers lived hand to mouth, so hiring at the eleventh hour was itself a mercy. Sitting at a king's right and left were the positions of highest honor. A ransom was the price paid to free a slave or captive. Jericho lay on the ascent to Jerusalem, crowded with Passover pilgrims.
context.canonical: The vineyard evokes Isaiah 5 and Israel as God's planting. The cup Jesus will drink recalls the cup of wrath in Isaiah 51 and Jeremiah 25. The ransom for many directly echoes Isaiah 53:11-12, the servant who bears the sin of many. The servant-greatness teaching is expanded in Philippians 2:5-11 and John 13.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the merit-based, envious heart and reveal the free generosity of God's grace, to redefine greatness as self-giving service, and to disclose the purpose of Jesus' death as a ransom for many.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parable describes God's sovereign generosity and warns against grumbling at grace; it prescribes contentment and gladness in God's goodness to others. The teaching on greatness is directly prescriptive: leadership among Jesus' people is service, modeled on His ransom.

debates.question: What is the main point of the parable of the workers?

views.label: God's free grace to the undeserving
views.case: The equal wage pictures the kingdom given by grace, not earned by length or amount of service; latecomers, Gentiles, or notorious sinners receive the same salvation as the long-laboring, which offends a merit mindset.

views.label: A warning against envy and entitlement
views.case: The focus falls on the grumblers whose eye is evil because the owner is good; the parable exposes the heart that resents God's generosity to others and reckons itself owed more, calling disciples to rejoice in grace rather than compare.

debates.bearing: Both belong together: the parable magnifies God's sovereign generosity and, in the same breath, warns the heart that would turn grace into wages and resent mercy shown to others.

study.originalLanguages: The parable turns on the landowner's freedom to be generous, and the grumblers' evil eye that resents it: the kingdom is grace, not wages. Against the disciples' scramble for the highest seats, Jesus declares that greatness belongs to the diakonos, the servant, and the first must be the slave of all. He grounds this in Himself: the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve and to give His life as a lytron, a ransom, in the place of many, the price that buys their freedom. And when two blind beggars cry out, He is moved with splanchnizomai, deep compassion, and opens their eyes so they follow Him. Grace, service, ransom, and mercy all flow from the same self-giving heart.

typology.fulfillment: God's people as His planting, the vineyard of Isaiah 5, now the sphere of His gracious call to labor.
The cup of God's judgment that Christ drains at the cross in the place of His people, Isaiah 51:17, Matthew 26:39.
The servant of Isaiah 53 who pours out His soul unto death and bears the sin of many, fulfilled in Christ's atoning death.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center as the Servant King. He foretells, for the third time and in fullest detail, that He will be handed over, mocked, flogged, and crucified, and on the third day raised. Against every human grasp for status He sets His own path downward: He did not come to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many, the price that liberates captives of sin. He is the Son of David whose compassion opens blind eyes, and the healed follow Him up the road to the cross. In Him, greatness and salvation alike are defined by self-giving love.
study.god: God is revealed as free, just, and lavishly generous. He keeps every agreement and wrongs no one, yet He will not be reduced to a paymaster who owes more for more; He gives grace as He pleases, and His goodness to latecomers is not injustice to the early but generosity that exposes envy. He prepares places in His kingdom by His own wise will. And His deepest self is shown in the Son of Man who serves and ransoms, for the generous landowner's heart is the heart of the God who gives His Son for many.

crossReferences.note: The servant who bears the sin of many, behind the ransom for many.
The vineyard of the LORD, the imagery underlying the parable's setting.
Christ taking the form of a servant and humbling Himself, the pattern of kingdom greatness.
The parallel passion prediction, ransom saying, and healing of blind Bartimaeus near Jericho.

study.oneStory: On the road up to Jerusalem, Jesus unmasks the heart that turns grace into wages and craves status, and He answers it with His own downward path: the Son of Man who serves and gives His life as a ransom for many. The kingdom is God's free generosity, and the last are made first. Two blind beggars cry for mercy, see, and follow, a living picture of everyone the ransom sets free. The whole story presses on toward the cross, where the price is paid and the vineyard is thrown open to all who come.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the vineyard workers chiefly teach?

options.text: God gives the kingdom by free grace, not as wages earned, which offends the merit-minded heart.
options.note: The equal wage and the owner's freedom to be generous picture grace, exposing the grumblers.

options.text: That everyone should be paid the same regardless of hours worked.
options.note: It uses wages as an image, but its subject is God's grace, not labor policy.

options.text: That the first workers were treated unfairly by the landowner.
options.note: The owner insists he wronged no one; they received exactly what was agreed.

options.text: That God rewards strictly according to how much we do.
options.note: The parable teaches the opposite, that God gives freely, not by strict measure of work.

quiz.q: How does Jesus define greatness in His kingdom?

options.text: The one who wants to be great must be a servant, and the first must be a slave of all.
options.note: Verses 26-27 invert worldly greatness into service.

options.text: Great leaders should use their authority wisely over others.
options.note: That is closer to the Gentile pattern Jesus rejects; greatness is serving, not ruling well.

options.text: Greatness comes from securing the most honored positions.
options.note: That is exactly the request Jesus corrects; the seats are not the path to greatness.

options.text: Greatness is unimportant and should never be desired at all.
options.note: Jesus redirects the desire for greatness toward service rather than forbidding it outright.

quiz.q: Why did the Son of Man come, according to verse 28?

options.text: Not to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many.
options.note: Verse 28 states the purpose of Jesus' death as a ransom.

options.text: To serve people by teaching and healing them.
options.note: He did serve in these ways, but the verse climaxes in giving His life as a ransom, which this omits.

options.text: To establish His right to be served as King.
options.note: The opposite; He came not to be served but to serve.

options.text: To show that self-sacrifice is unnecessary for greatness.
options.note: His self-sacrifice is the very definition and ground of greatness here.

quiz.q: What marks the two blind men outside Jericho?

options.text: They persist in crying to the Son of David for mercy, are healed, and follow Jesus.
options.note: Verses 30-34 show their persistent faith, healing, and discipleship.

options.text: They ask Jesus for money as beggars by the road.
options.note: They were beggars, but they asked for sight and mercy, not money.

options.text: They are silenced by the crowd and give up.
options.note: The crowd tried to silence them, but they cried out all the louder.

options.text: They are healed but then walk away from Jesus.
options.note: Having received sight, they followed Him.

questions: The all-day workers resented the owner's generosity to others. Where does grace shown to someone else stir envy or a sense of being owed in your heart?
God asks, are you envious because I am generous? How would gladness in His goodness to others change the way you view your own lot?
Jesus measures greatness by service. In your home, work, or church, where is He calling you to lead by serving rather than by being served?
The Son of Man gave His life as a ransom for you. How does dwelling on that price reshape your grip on status and your willingness to give?
The blind men cried out all the louder when told to be quiet. What need should you keep bringing to Jesus even when others would hush you?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts add For many are called, but few are chosen, as at Matthew 22:14; the saying that frames the parable is the last will be first, and the first will be last.
Some manuscripts add and be baptized with the baptism with which I am baptized, drawn from Mark 10:38-39; the cup Jesus speaks of is His coming suffering and death.

The source joins slave and the ransom saying with a dash; it is rendered here with a comma for a clean, dash-free reading, so that verse 28 flows on: just as the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve.

Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. The em dash at the end of verse 27 was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
